does: This paper introduces transcoders as a tool for mechanistic interpretability of language models, addressing the challenge that MLP layers obscure interpretable feature circuits. Transcoders approximate dense MLP activations with wider, sparsely-activating MLPs that replace nonlinearities with interpretable structure. The authors develop a weights-based circuit analysis method that traces information flow through MLP sublayers by decomposing circuits into input-dependent and input-invariant components. They successfully train transcoders on models ranging from 120M to 1.4B parameters and demonstrate performance comparable to or exceeding sparse autoencoders (SAEs) in reconstructing MLP outputs while maintaining interpretability.
connects: This work builds directly on the mechanistic interpretability agenda of circuit analysis, which seeks to isolate sparse computational subgraphs implementing specific behaviors in neural networks. It responds to limitations in prior work using sparse autoencoders for feature discovery, which struggle with transformer MLP layers because learned features are opaque linear combinations of many nonlinear neurons. The paper connects to broader efforts in interpretability to move beyond attribution methods and neuron-level analysis toward understanding actual computation. It leverages ideas from model compression and knowledge distillation, adapting them to create interpretable approximations rather than simply efficient ones, and extends circuit analysis techniques previously developed primarily for attention layers to the previously intractable MLP setting.
enables: This work opens pathways for detailed mechanistic understanding of how language models process information through their densest layers. By making MLP circuits interpretable and sparse, transcoders enable researchers to trace specific computations across full transformer depth, potentially revealing how models implement reasoning, factual recall, or other capabilities. The interpretable decomposition of MLPs could facilitate better mechanistic explanations of model behavior, improve targeted model editing or steering, and support safety-relevant analysis of how models compute harmful outputs. The method scales to larger models and could support investigating interpretability trade-offs in scaling laws, while interpretable transcoders themselves might inform better model architectures or training procedures that naturally induce sparsity.
